message:
fix(policy): anti-thrash cooldown + canary budget stays in canary mode

Address rapid canary↔fallback oscillation on active machines and
excessive notification noise from special-location monitoring:

Policy engine (policy.rs):
- Add min_fallback_secs config field (default 300s) requiring a minimum
  cooldown period before recovery from FallbackSafe is attempted,
  preventing bursty workloads from causing rapid mode oscillation
- Canary budget exhaustion no longer triggers FallbackSafe — deletions
  are simply capped (Keep) while the engine stays in Canary mode.
  Entering FallbackSafe after a successful canary run was
  counterproductive since recovery requires consecutive clean guard
  windows which could take hours on active machines
- Increase default recovery_clean_windows 3→30 and
  calibration_breach_windows 3→15 for more stable mode transitions
  in production
- Add eprintln transition logging for fallback entry and recovery
- Fix enter_fallback() to capture pre-transition mode before mutation

Daemon loop (loop_main.rs):
- Simplify special-location notification to fire only on level change
  (removes the 5-minute cooldown + escalation-only logic that was
  still producing oscillation noise)
- Add rate-limited policy-rejection logging in executor thread (once
  per 5 minutes) to reduce log spam on busy machines

CLI (cli_app.rs):
- Expose policy_mode in status JSON output for monitoring

Tests:
- All test configs set min_fallback_secs: 0 for instant recovery in
  unit/integration tests
- Updated canary budget exhaustion assertions to expect Canary mode
  instead of FallbackSafe
- replay_observe_canary_enforce_lifecycle uses explicit PolicyConfig
  with recovery_clean_windows: 3 for backward-compatible assertion

Version: 0.2.5 → 0.2.7

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -1,6 +1,6 @@
 [package]
 name = "storage_ballast_helper"
-version = "0.2.5"
+version = "0.2.7"
 edition = "2024"
 license = "MIT"
 description = "System service that prevents disk-full scenarios from coding agent swarms via ballast files, artifact scanning, and special location monitoring."
